platform.name: VulnLab
platform.description: A hands-on cybersecurity training platform with intentionally vulnerable lab environments.
platform.flag_format: FLAG{...} — cryptographically tied to your active session. If your session expired, re-exploit to get a new flag.
platform.instance_ttl: Lab instances expire after 5 minutes of inactivity. Heartbeats from the lab page keep them alive.
platform.flag_submission: Use the orange flag button (bottom-right of any lab page) to submit. Select the correct objective first.
platform.automated_tools: Manual methodology required. Automated scanners prohibited unless lab explicitly allows them.

labs.title: Path Traversal
labs.what_it_is: Path traversal lets an attacker read files outside the intended directory by using ../ sequences in a filename parameter.
labs.real_world_impact: Attackers read /etc/passwd, config files, private keys, or source code from servers.
tiered_hints: Think about what controls which file gets returned when you download something.
The file parameter in the request is passed directly to the filesystem. What happens if you change its value?
Try adding ../ sequences to navigate up directories. Where might a sensitive file live on a Linux system?
labs.stuck_advice: Intercept the download request with browser DevTools or Burp Suite. Look at what parameter is sent and experiment with its value.
labs.defense: Sanitize file paths server-side. Use os.path.basename() or resolve the real path and verify it starts with the allowed base directory.

labs.title: SSRF
labs.what_it_is: SSRF tricks the server into making HTTP requests to destinations you control, including internal services the server can reach but you cannot.
labs.real_world_impact: SSRF was behind the Capital One breach (2019). Attackers reached AWS metadata endpoints to steal credentials.
tiered_hints: Find the parameter that tells the server which URL to fetch for stock data.
The server will fetch any URL you give it — including http://localhost/...
Once you reach the admin panel via SSRF, look for a delete action and replay that URL too.
labs.stuck_advice: Intercept the stock check request. The stockApi parameter is your entry point. Start with http://localhost/admin.
labs.defense: Allowlist permitted outbound domains. Never pass user-controlled values directly to an HTTP client.

labs.title: File Upload
labs.what_it_is: The server accepts uploaded files without properly validating their type or content, allowing executable code to be uploaded and triggered.
labs.real_world_impact: File upload vulnerabilities allow remote code execution — the highest severity class of web vulnerability.
tiered_hints: Try uploading a normal image first to understand how the upload works and where files are stored.
The server checks the Content-Type header, not the actual file content. What happens if you change that header?
Upload a PHP file while spoofing the Content-Type to image/jpeg. Then navigate to the file's public path to trigger it.
labs.stuck_advice: Use Burp Suite to intercept the upload request. Modify the filename extension and Content-Type independently.
labs.defense: Validate file content (magic bytes), not just the extension or MIME header. Store uploads outside the web root.

labs.title: SQL Injection
labs.what_it_is: User input is embedded directly into SQL queries without sanitisation, letting attackers manipulate the query logic.
labs.real_world_impact: SQLi can dump entire databases, bypass login, and in some configurations execute OS commands.
tiered_hints: Find an input that gets embedded in a database query — product filters, search boxes, login fields.
Try a single quote ' to break the query and see if the error reveals database details.
For UNION attacks, first determine the number of columns, then match their types before extracting data.
labs.stuck_advice: Start with a quote character to see if the input is injectable. Read error messages carefully — they tell you the database type.
labs.defense: Use parameterised queries / prepared statements. Never concatenate user input into SQL strings.

labs.title: XSS
labs.what_it_is: User input is rendered in the browser without encoding, letting attackers inject JavaScript that executes in other users' browsers.
labs.real_world_impact: XSS enables session hijacking, credential theft, and full account takeover.
tiered_hints: Find where user input is reflected back in the page and look at how it sits in the HTML — is it inside a tag, an attribute, or a script?
The injection context changes which payload works. Inside a tag: use <script>. Inside an attribute: use an event handler like onerror=.
Try the simplest possible payload first: <script>alert(1)</script>. If it's filtered, try an event-handler variant.
labs.stuck_advice: Open browser DevTools, inspect where your input appears in the DOM, then craft your payload for that exact context.
labs.defense: HTML-encode all output. Use Content-Security-Policy headers. Never use innerHTML with user data.

labs.title: Mobile Binary Analysis
labs.what_it_is: Android APKs are ZIP archives containing compiled code. Developers sometimes leave API keys, credentials, or internal URLs hardcoded in the binary.
labs.real_world_impact: Hardcoded secrets in production apps have leaked AWS keys, payment credentials, and internal APIs.
tiered_hints: An APK is just a ZIP file. Start by extracting it and exploring its contents.
The DEX file contains compiled Java/Kotlin code. Use JADX or apktool to decompile it to readable source.
Search the decompiled source for strings like 'password', 'key', 'secret', 'api', 'http'. They're often in resource files or constants.
labs.stuck_advice: Run: unzip app.apk -d app_extracted. Then use jadx-gui to open the APK directly and search for credential patterns.
labs.defense: Never hardcode secrets. Use Android Keystore, environment injection at runtime, or a secrets manager.

advanced: Combine multiple labs into an attack chain

out_of_scope: Flags or flag values for any instance
Target usernames or passwords from active instances
Exact payloads that solve a lab step-by-step
Questions about other students' progress or data
General cybersecurity questions unrelated to platform labs
out_of_scope_response: I can only answer questions about this platform and its labs. I won't provide flags, credentials, exact exploit payloads, or other students' data. Please ask about a lab (for example: 'SSRF hint') or platform topics like 'flags', 'sessions', or 'tools'.
fallback_response: I can help with platform queries ('flags', 'sessions', 'tools', 'vpn') or explain vulnerabilities like 'SSRF', 'SQL Injection', 'XSS', 'File Upload', etc. Ask for a 'hint', 'defense', or 'impact'!
